Course Title: AI Risk in Financial Forecasting and Reporting
Description: Specific risks when AI influences financial decisions.
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic understanding of financial reporting principles and a general familiarity with how AI tools are used in business settings, though no technical or programming knowledge is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key risks associated with using AI in financial forecasting and explain how these risks can affect the accuracy and reliability of financial reporting. You will also apply practical strategies to recognise, assess, and manage AI-related risks within your organisation's financial decision-making processes.

lessons.lesson title: AI Risk in Financial Forecasting and Reporting
lessons.Description: This lesson explores the specific risks that arise when artificial intelligence is used to support financial forecasting and reporting within organisations. You will learn how AI systems can introduce errors, biases, and blind spots into financial decision-making, often in ways that are difficult to detect without the right knowledge. The lesson is designed for beginners, so no prior technical expertise is required, just a willingness to understand how these tools can go wrong and why it matters. By the end, you will be better equipped to identify warning signs, ask the right questions, and apply appropriate caution when AI-generated insights inform financial outcomes.
lessons.content: Specific risks when AI influences financial decisions.

slides.slidetitle: Welcome to AI Risk in Financial Forecasting
slides.subtitle: Understanding the Stakes When AI Influences Financial Decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• AI is reshaping financial forecasting and reporting across industries
• Real risks emerge when models make decisions humans don't fully understand
• Errors can compound quickly, affecting critical business outcomes
• From credit scoring to earnings projections, AI is already embedded in finance
• This course covers the specific risks that matter most for financial professionals

slides.subtitle: How AI Functions in Financial Forecasting and Reporting
slides.slidecontent:
• Processes large financial datasets at unprecedented speed
• Identifies patterns and trends invisible to human analysis
• Generates automated forecasts and projections
• Handles routine reporting tasks without human intervention
• Provides real-time anomaly detection and alerts

slides.subtitle: When Poor Input Data Creates Financial Forecast Failures
slides.slidecontent:
• Garbage in, garbage out principle applies directly to AI models
• Outdated training data produces forecasts based on obsolete conditions
• Incomplete datasets create blind spots in model predictions
• Data gaps from system migrations or missing records skew results
• Quality issues multiply when models rely on flawed foundation data

slides.subtitle: How Past Inequalities Shape Current AI Predictions
slides.slidecontent:
• Historical data reflects past economic inequalities and market distortions
• Models trained on biased samples produce systematically unfair outcomes
• Certain demographics, regions, or business sectors become underrepresented
• Boom-period training data fails to predict downturn risks accurately
• Bias often remains invisible until financial damage occurs

slides.subtitle: When AI Performance Deteriorates Over Time
slides.slidecontent:
• AI models lose accuracy as real-world conditions change from training scenarios
• Market shifts make historical patterns less relevant for current predictions
• Models become overconfident in stable conditions, underestimate volatility
• Performance degradation happens gradually, making detection difficult
• Without regular retraining, models provide false confidence in outdated assumptions

slides.subtitle: Essential Review Processes for AI-Generated Financial Data
slides.slidecontent:
• Require human review before acting on AI-generated forecasts
• Establish clear escalation procedures for unusual AI predictions
• Maintain detailed audit trails for all AI outputs and decisions
• Set accuracy thresholds that trigger manual verification
• Train staff to recognize unreliable AI outputs and red flags

slides.subtitle: Ongoing Strategies to Maintain AI Reliability
slides.slidecontent:
• Test models regularly against historical data to verify accuracy
• Retrain models frequently with current, representative data
• Document all model assumptions and limitations clearly
• Cross-validate AI outputs with independent data sources
• Rotate model versions to prevent over-reliance on single approaches

slides.subtitle: Next Steps for Managing AI Risk in Your Organization
slides.slidecontent:
• Review current AI policies to identify accountability gaps
• Verify all AI forecasts against reliable source data before decisions
• Ensure human expertise remains central to financial judgment
• Document every instance where AI influences reported figures
• Understand and communicate the limitations of your AI tools

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI Risk in Financial Forecasting and Reporting. AI is already deeply embedded in modern finance, generating credit scores, earnings projections, and financial reports at unprecedented speed and scale. But this power comes with real risk. When AI models make decisions that people don't fully understand, things can go wrong quickly. Small errors can ripple outward and compound into serious problems before anyone notices. This course is designed for financial professionals who need to understand these risks clearly and practically. We'll focus on what actually matters when AI influences financial decision-making, without getting lost in technical details.

Before diving into risks, let's establish what AI actually does in financial forecasting and reporting. AI excels at processing enormous volumes of financial data far faster than human teams can manage. It identifies patterns and trends buried deep in data that might be invisible to human analysis. AI systems generate projections and predictions, automate routine reporting tasks, and flag unusual activity in real time rather than waiting for quarterly reviews. These are genuinely powerful capabilities, which is precisely why understanding the associated risks is so critical for financial professionals.

The foundation of any AI system is data quality, and this is where many financial forecasting problems begin. The principle of garbage in, garbage out applies perfectly here. If you feed an AI model flawed, outdated, or incomplete data, the forecasts it produces will reflect those same flaws. Imagine using training data from before a major economic shift - the model has no awareness the world has changed, so its predictions reflect a reality that no longer exists. Data gaps from system migrations or missing records create additional blind spots that can seriously skew results.

Historical bias presents one of the most insidious risks in AI financial modeling. When you train an AI system on historical financial data, you're also feeding it all the inequalities and market distortions embedded in that history. This leads to systematically unfair outcomes in credit scores, loan approvals, and risk assessments. Certain demographics, regions, or business sectors become disadvantaged without anyone realizing it's happening. Models trained during economic booms may dramatically underestimate risk during downturns. The troubling aspect of bias is that it often remains hidden until financial damage has already occurred.

Even well-built AI models face the challenge of model drift - the gradual deterioration of performance as real-world conditions change from training scenarios. Financial markets evolve, economic conditions shift, and regulatory environments change, but AI models continue operating based on historical patterns that may no longer be relevant. Models can become overconfident during stable periods and fail to recognize emerging volatility. This degradation happens gradually, making it difficult to detect until accuracy has significantly declined. Without regular monitoring and retraining, organizations may operate with false confidence in outdated model assumptions.

Managing AI risk requires implementing robust human oversight controls. Never act on AI-generated forecasts without human review - AI should inform decision-making, not replace human judgment. Establish clear escalation procedures when predictions fall outside expected ranges, and maintain detailed audit trails for all AI outputs. Set specific accuracy thresholds that automatically trigger manual verification. Most importantly, train your staff to recognize when AI outputs may be unreliable, including understanding common failure patterns and red flags that indicate model problems.

Effective model management requires ongoing attention to maintain AI reliability over time. Regularly test your models against historical data to verify continued accuracy and detect emerging bias. Retrain models frequently with current, representative data to prevent drift and ensure relevance. Document all model assumptions and limitations clearly so users understand what the system can and cannot do. Cross-validate AI outputs with independent data sources, and consider rotating between different model versions to prevent over-reliance on any single approach to forecasting.

As we conclude, here are your key action items for managing AI risk in financial forecasting. Start by reviewing your organization's current AI policies to identify any accountability gaps. Always verify AI forecasts against reliable source data before making decisions. Ensure human expertise remains central to financial judgment - AI should augment, not replace, professional insight. Document every instance where AI influences reported figures to maintain proper audit trails. Finally, make sure everyone understands and can communicate the limitations of your AI tools. These practical steps will help you harness AI's benefits while protecting against its risks.

Who is this for: Financial analysts, accounting managers, finance directors, CFOs, budget planners, financial controllers, business analysts, treasury managers
Team or Dept this is for: Finance, Accounting, Treasury, Financial Planning & Analysis, Executive Leadership, Risk Management
